retrieved_docs.title: Diabetic Embryopathy -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Optimizing health outcomes in pregnancies complicated by diabetes necessitates a coordinated, interprofessional approach. Early diagnosis, timely intervention, and ongoing management are best achieved through comprehensive care delivered by a collaborative team of healthcare professionals. Due to the complex effects of diabetes on both maternal and neonatal health, a multidisciplinary strategy is vital.

retrieved_docs.title: Gestational Diabetes -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Proper control of maternal glucose levels significantly reduces GDM risks, including macrosomia and neonatal hypoglycemia. The risk of preeclampsia decreases from 18% to 12% with treatment. [1] If GDM interventions, including dietary modifications or pharmacologic therapy, are implemented early enough, progression to type 2 diabetes in 10 years is reduced by 35% to 40%. Furthermore, even small reductions in BMI can result in a reduced risk for diabetes by 25%. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Gestational Diabetes -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is typically defined as hyperglycemia that is diagnosed or develops during pregnancy. GDM is often divided into classes, primarily diet-controlled GDM (class A1GDM) or GDM requiring pharmacologic treatment of hyperglycemia (class A2GDM). [1] The benefits of identifying GDM have long been established, with several studies demonstrating that women diagnosed with GDM are at high risk for developing type 2 diabetes long-term and that antepartum GDM treatment reduces adverse pregnancy outcomes. However, a consensus on the diagnostic criteria for GDM or the optimal timing for pregnancy screening has not been reached. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Gestational Diabetes -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Over several decades, GDM management approaches have continued to evolve due to alarmingly high perinatal mortality rates among women with diabetes during pregnancy. A significant leap came in 1964 with the publication of O’Sullivan and Mahan’s landmark study that defined specific diagnostic criteria for GDM in the United States. Their criteria, based on a 100-gram 3-hour oral glucose tolerance test, were designed to identify women at increased risk, aiming to correlate maternal hyperglycemia with adverse perinatal outcomes. Subsequent validation studies underscored the importance of these thresholds in predicting both immediate complications and long-term health risks for mothers and their children. [1] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetes -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Those with gestational diabetes are at a higher risk for cesarean delivery and chronic hypertension. Pregnant patients with T2DM generally have a better prognosis in terms of neonatal and pregnancy complications compared to those with T1DM. Generally, neonates of DM mothers will present with hypoglycemia and macrosomia. [52]

retrieved_docs.title: Gestational Diabetes -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Nurses are integral to the frontline delivery of care; they are responsible for monitoring patient adherence to treatment regimens and promptly identifying and reporting any adverse events or complications. Pharmacists contribute through medication management, ensuring accurate dosing of insulin or other antihyperglycemic agents, reconciling medications to prevent interactions, and counseling patients on proper administration and adherence to prescribed therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetic Embryopathy -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The perinatal mortality rate in diabetic pregnancies is approximately 2.5 to 9 times higher than in the general population. Several studies have shown that type 2 diabetes is associated with a higher rate of perinatal deaths than type 1 diabetes, mainly due to additional risk factors such as hypertension, advanced maternal age, obesity, and multiparity. However, data from French studies indicate that stillbirths are more frequently observed in patients with type 1 diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetic Embryopathy -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy outcomes are significantly influenced by maternal diet during pregnancy. Preconceptional assessment of maternal risk factors and blood glucose levels is essential for accurate prognostication. Maintaining strict glycemic control remains the most effective strategy for preventing or reducing the incidence of congenital anomalies. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetic Embryopathy -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Certain differences have been observed between type 1 and type 2 diabetes in pregnant women. [23] Type 1 diabetes has been associated with younger mothers at the time of pregnancy. There is also a higher rate of reported preterm deliveries and large-for-gestational-age births. In contrast, type 2 diabetes is typically associated with older maternal age, a higher maternal body mass index (BMI), and a greater burden of comorbidities at the time of pregnancy. In addition, type 2 diabetes is also associated with an increased risk of neonatal death compared to type 1 diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetes Mellitus Screening -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: There is compelling evidence that early treatment of diabetes can reduce microvascular complications of diabetes. [15] The prompt treatment of the disease has significantly improved mortality, especially related to vascular disease. [5] The evidence for the impact of screening for diabetes is increasing. It is considered to be 1 of the more straightforward ways to prevent and reduce morbidity and mortality of this disease. Excess mortality related to type 2 diabetes results from cardiovascular events, and early screening has the potential to allow for early intervention. The UKPDS demonstrated a 14% reduction in myocardial infarctions for every 1% decrease in HbA1c value. [16] Gestational diabetes is an identified cause of several adverse outcomes during pregnancy, like pre-eclampsia, macrosomia, birth trauma, perinatal mortality, and neonatal cardiac, respiratory, and metabolic complications. [17] There is also a long-term risk of developing type 1/type 2 diabetes mellitus and cardiovascular disease in the mother, as well as metabolic syndrome, obesity, hypertension, and impaired glucose tolerance in the child. Identifying this early and prompt management has a positive impact on the health of the mother as well as the child. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Gestational Diabetes -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Since then, the evolution of diagnostic criteria for GDM has reflected ongoing scientific inquiry and shifting standards in maternal-fetal medicine. From the adaptation of glucose thresholds by international bodies like the World Health Organization (WHO) to the endorsement of comprehensive criteria by the International Association of the Diabetes and Pregnancy Study Groups (IADPSG) in 2013, GDM management approaches continue to evolve. These advancements highlight not only the complexity of diagnosing GDM but also underscore the critical need for evidence-based guidelines that mitigate risks for both mothers and infants. As diagnostic strategies continue to refine, ongoing research aims to optimize maternal and perinatal outcomes while addressing the diverse healthcare needs of pregnant women globally. [1] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Gestational Diabetes -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: However, studies have demonstrated that the most significant associations for GDM risk were family history, previous GDM, Southeast Asian ethnicity, parity, and high BMI. Therefore, ethnic and racial differences in these risk factors for GDM development should also be considered. [7] Additionally, recent studies have shown that early screening for gestational diabetes does not prevent adverse outcomes and may even lead to worse outcomes in select patients. These findings highlight the complexity of GDM pathogenesis and management. Consequently, the effectiveness of the early gestational diabetes screening recommendation is debatable and requires additional studies. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Diabetic Protocols For Treat and Release -- Introduction -- Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM)
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperglycemia first detected during pregnancy is classified as gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM). Although it can occur anytime during pregnancy, GDM generally affects pregnant women during the second and third trimesters. According to the American Diabetes Association (ADA), GDM complicates 7% of all pregnancies. Women with GDM and their offspring have an increased risk of developing type 2 diabetes mellitus in the future. Types of Therapies [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Gestational Diabetes -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Effective interprofessional communication is critical to coordinating care seamlessly across disciplines. Innovative models, such as pharmacy or nurse practitioner-led diabetic clinics, exemplify this collaborative effort by providing specialized diabetes education, continuous monitoring of blood glucose levels, and facilitating timely referrals to specialists when necessary. These clinics not only alleviate the workload of obstetricians but also ensure that pregnant women with gestational diabetes receive comprehensive, coordinated care that addresses both their immediate needs and long-term health risks. This collaborative approach not only enhances patient safety by minimizing medication errors and optimizing therapy but also improves overall team performance and patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Gestational Diabetes -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The management of gestational diabetes demands a collaborative interprofessional team approach to ensure patient-centered care and optimize outcomes. Physicians, advanced practitioners, nurses, pharmacists, and other health professionals each play distinct yet interconnected roles in this endeavor. Physicians lead the clinical decision-making process, diagnosing gestational diabetes, formulating treatment plans, and monitoring maternal and fetal health throughout pregnancy. Advanced practitioners often manage day-to-day patient care, conducting regular assessments, adjusting treatment protocols as needed, and providing patient education on glucose monitoring, dietary modifications, and insulin administration.

retrieved_docs.title: Gestational Diabetes -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: GDM is a common complication in pregnancy. The International Diabetes Federation recently estimated that globally, 1 in 6 live births had a GDM diagnosis. [3] In the United States, approximately 7% of pregnancies were complicated by diabetes of any type, with 86% of those cases being pregnancies complicated by GDM. [1] The estimated prevalence of GDM in Europe is 10.9%. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2306
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY is a subcategory within the broader group of Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium. Among the many obstetric complications encompassed by this larger category, diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium represents a specific type of complication. This relationship underscores that gestational diabetes and related disorders of glucose metabolism are recognized as important pregnancy‑related complications and are included in the overall spectrum of conditions that can affect maternal and fetal outcomes during pregnancy, delivery, and the postpartum period. Effective identification and management of these glucose‑related complications are essential components of comprehensive obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1798
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy complications (مضاعفات الحمل) encompass a range of conditions, including fetal disorder (اضطراب جنيني), threatened abortion (تهديد بالإجهاض), and gestational diabetes (داء السكري أثناء الحمل). Both threatened abortion and gestational diabetes are recognized as specific types of pregnancy complications. Clinically, optional termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري) is associated with the management of gestational diabetes and also with threatened abortion, indicating that termination may be considered as a therapeutic option in these particular obstetric scenarios.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2372
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance that arises during pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is recognized as a specific type of complication within the broader category of complications of pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium. This condition represents a distinct obstetric complication where altered glucose metabolism adversely affects maternal and fetal health, underscoring the importance of monitoring and managing glucose levels throughout gestation, delivery, and the postpartum period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2809
retrieved_docs.content: Elective termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري) is clinically associated with several obstetric conditions, including gestational diabetes (داء السكري أثناء الحمل), threatened miscarriage (تهديد بالإجهاض), and iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الحديد). These associations highlight the importance of evaluating and managing metabolic, hematologic, and early‑pregnancy complications when counseling patients about elective pregnancy termination, ensuring comprehensive risk assessment and optimal maternal health outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium is classified under the broader category Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery, indicating that it is a subset of the clinical indications guiding obstetric management. Within this group of obstetric complications, diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium is recognized as a specific type of complication, highlighting that gestational diabetes and related glucose intolerance are important conditions to consider when evaluating and treating patients during pregnancy, labor, and the postpartum period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 153
retrieved_docs.content: hemorrhagic and metabolic disorders, and pregnancy‑loss entities within a hierarchical framework of obstetric complications. This structure guides clinicians in recognizing, categorizing, and managing the diverse factors—ranging from prior uterine scar and threatened labor to placental hemorrhage and gestational diabetes—that influence maternal and fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1794
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes (مرض السكري) encompasses the specific condition described as “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium,” which is a recognized form of diabetes. Diabetes is clinically associated with retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية) and more specifically with diabetic retinopathy (اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ). Both diabetes and diabetic retinopathy are also clinically associated with end‑stage renal disease (مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية). These connections illustrate how diabetes can lead to multiple organ complications, linking retinal pathology and kidney failure, and emphasize the need for integrated monitoring and management of patients with diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2434
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium is a broad category that encompasses specific maternal health problems such as hypertension and/or vomiting complicating pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium, as well as diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance occurring during pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium. Both hypertension‑related vomiting and gestational diabetes represent distinct subtypes of the overall complications associated with the perinatal period, highlighting the need for careful monitoring and targeted management of these conditions to protect maternal and fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2872
retrieved_docs.content: Elective termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري) is clinically associated with both gestational diabetes (داء السكري أثناء الحمل) and threatened abortion (تهديد بالإجهاض). In clinical practice, the presence of gestational diabetes or a threatened abortion may influence the consideration of an elective termination, and healthcare providers should be aware of these associations when counseling patients and planning management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2903
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium—commonly referred to as gestational diabetes—is recognized as a specific form of مرض السكري (diabetes). مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية (retinal disorder), exemplified by diabetic retinopathy. This relationship underscores the need for careful ocular monitoring and management of retinal health in patients with diabetes, particularly those experiencing gestational diabetes during and after pregnancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2061
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal disorder (اضطراب جنيني), premature rupture of membranes (تمزق الأغشية الباكر), threatened preterm labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر) and gestational diabetes (داء السكري أثناء الحمل) are all classified as pregnancy complications (مضاعفات الحمل). Both gestational diabetes and premature rupture of membranes are clinically associated with the use of a low transverse cesarean section, indicating that patients with these conditions may be more likely to require this specific surgical delivery approach. Recognizing these obstetric conditions as part of the broader category of pregnancy complications helps clinicians anticipate appropriate interventions, such as low transverse cesarean section, and tailor management to improve maternal and fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1950
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all recognized as specific forms or presentations that map to مرض السكري (diabetes). The condition described as “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium” is also identified as a manifestation of مرض السكري, emphasizing the impact of diabetes on maternal and perinatal health. Norgestrel is contraindicated in patients with مرض السكري, indicating that this contraceptive medication should be avoided in individuals with the disease. Additionally, اضطراب في الشبكية (retinal disorder) is clinically associated with مرض السكري, reflecting the well‑known ocular complications of diabetes. Collectively, these relationships illustrate the diverse neonatal and developmental expressions of diabetes, its complications during pregnancy, medication safety considerations, and its association with retinal pathology.

retrieved_docs.content: unspecified trimester”). Metabolic and circulatory disorders such as hypertension (including the subset with vomiting) and diabetes/abnormal glucose tolerance are represented as diseases of the circulatory system that complicate pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium. Together, these entities illustrate a hierarchical knowledge network in which prior cesarean‑section scars, pelvic‑organ and cervical abnormalities, hypertensive and metabolic disorders, infectious complications, and bleeding manifestations are interrelated as risk factors, diagnostic categories, and management indications that guide risk assessment, monitoring, and therapeutic decision‑making for optimal maternal and fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 144
retrieved_docs.content: **diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium**, and **Indications for care in pregnancy, labor, and delivery**. **Cervical shortening** is classified under **Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium** and also under **other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother**. It is an obstetric cervical abnormality divided into **Cervical shortening, second trimester**, **Cervical shortening, third trimester**, and **Cervical shortening, unspecified trimester**; each trimester‑specific form is further classified as **Early or threatened labor**, emphasizing its association with premature or threatened onset of labor. The unspecified‑trimester form is mapped to **short cervical length in pregnancy**, indicating that a reduced cervical length at any gestational stage is recognized as a short cervical length. **Cervixinsufficiëntie als indicatie voor zorg bij moeder**

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 200
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium encompass a wide range of obstetric conditions that affect the mother, fetus and labor course. Early or threatened labor (مخاض كاذب, “false labor”) is a clinical presentation that includes cervical shortening, which is recorded as a measurable obstetric finding (short cervical length) in the second trimester, third trimester or an unspecified trimester. Cervical shortening is therefore a subtype of early or threatened labor and is linked to cervical stenosis, both classified as obstetric cervical abnormalities. Maternal systemic disorders that complicate pregnancy include hypertension complicating pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium (ضغط الدم) and its variant hypertension ± vomiting, as well as diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance. Hypertension is positioned as a circulatory‑system disease that can co‑occur with vomiting and influences both maternal and fetal outcomes. Pregnancy‑related anatomic and clinical findings such
